Title: Understanding end-user acceptance of ‘new sanita4on’: the path towards a circular urban water system
Abstract/Summary: The transition towards circular and water-wise cities requires the implementation of sanitation systems that make use of water-saving techniques, as well as resourceful use of waste streams. Previous research has identified strategies that professionals involved in the development and implementation of such ‘new sanitation systems’ can deploy to legitimize the innovation to end-users and to improve acceptance. This research explored the current state of end-user acceptance and the legitimation strategies deployed by professionals in two new sanitation projects within KWR’s ANCHOR project: the neighborhoods ‘SUPERLOCAL’ in Kerkrade (NL) and ‘De Nieuwe Dokken’ in Ghent (BE). This research aimed to explore the role that legitimation strategies have in shaping end-user acceptance.
Main Research Question: What role do legitimation strategies play in shaping end-user acceptance of new sanitation systems?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on innovative sanitation systems that aim to improve water management and promote sustainable practices in urban environments.
Keywords: new sanitation, end-user acceptance, legitimation strategies, circular urban water system, KWR ANCHOR project
Specific Methods: Coded interview transcripts

Title: A PROTOCOL FOR SAMPLING ECOHYDROLOGICAL TRAITS IN NEPAL’S TERAI ARC LANDSCAPE
Abstract/Summary: The Terai Arc Landscape (TAL) of Nepal is a global priority conservation area for the Bengal Tiger. This Master's thesis aims to develop a protocol for the systematic collection of data on Leaf Area Index, Fractional Vegetation Cover, and Surface Soil Moisture, which are key ecohydrological parameters. The protocol demonstrated its ability to capture reliable data across various vegetation types and explored a smartphone-based method for LAI measurement. The study also validated satellite products against in-situ data, revealing significant relationships, although the satellite products tended to underestimate values. The findings have important implications for conservation efforts in the TAL.
Main Research Question: How can a protocol be developed to systematically collect reliable ecohydrological data in the Terai Arc Landscape to support conservation efforts for the Bengal Tiger?
Justification for SDG: This research contributes to the conservation of critical habitats for the Bengal Tiger, addressing biodiversity loss and promoting sustainable land use.
Keywords: Leaf Area Index, Fractional Vegetation Cover, Surface Soil Moisture, ecohydrology, conservation
Specific Methods: Protocol design, field data collection, satellite data validation

Title: Development of a Global National Water Management Index
Abstract/Summary: This thesis addresses the global challenges of water demand, availability, quality, and extreme events by proposing a set of water-related indicators for monitoring Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) on a global scale. The aim is to develop a National Water Management Index that integrates the water-energy-food nexus into a comprehensive framework. The methodology includes system analysis and the development of an indicator framework. The main findings highlight the interlinkages between water, energy, and food, and provide insights into the performance of countries regarding water management.
Main Research Question: How can a National Water Management Index be developed to monitor water-related indicators for the Sustainable Development Goals on a global scale?
Justification for SDG: This SDG is relevant as the thesis focuses on water management and the development of indicators to monitor water-related challenges and progress.
Keywords: Water management, SDGs, indicators, water-energy-food nexus, National Water Management Index
Specific Methods: System analysis, Indicator framework development
Theories: City Blueprint® Approach, National Blueprint Framework

Title: Optimizing the water allocation system in the Dutch region by adopting the WEAP model
Abstract/Summary: Water scarcity has increased worldwide due to climate change, rapid population growth, socio-economic development, and environmental degradation leading to water resource crises. A critical challenge in combating droughts is optimizing water resources allocation to meet ecological, economic, and social needs in uncertain future climate change conditions. This research used a Water Evaluation and Planning (WEAP) model to obtain insights for water resources management strategies for the municipality of Laarbeek, the Netherlands. The scenario analysis showed that the reuse of wastewater for irrigation is feasible in the study region, improving the quality of the River Aa by reducing pollutant loads.
Main Research Question: How can the pressure on the groundwater resources be alleviated by making new connections between water users and water supplies?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on optimizing water allocation and management to address water scarcity and improve water quality.
Keywords: Water Evaluation and Planning (WEAP) model, water allocation, reuse wastewater, water quality
Specific Methods: Scenario analysis, WEAP model setup

Title: Flexibility Increasing Solutions for Groundwater Extractions A First Exploration for Solutions
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores solutions to increase flexibility in groundwater extraction. The aim is to identify and classify various methods that can enhance groundwater management in rural environments. A case study approach is employed, focusing on shallow groundwater extraction and riverbank filtration. The findings reveal several effective strategies, including surface water injection and aquifer storage recovery, which can improve the efficiency and sustainability of groundwater extraction practices.
Main Research Question: What solutions can be implemented to increase flexibility in groundwater extraction?
Justification for SDG: This research directly addresses the sustainable management of water resources, aiming to improve groundwater extraction methods and ensure water availability.
Keywords: groundwater extraction, flexibility, Sustainability, Water management
Specific Methods: Case study, Solution classification, Testing efficiency

Title: The risk of opportunistic pathogens in Dutch drinking water – A newly derived DALY approach for assessing the risk of Legionella pneumophila
Supervisor: Kees van Leeuwen, Jerry van Dijk
Abstract/Summary: The disease burden of waterborne pathogens is a growing concern within the Dutch water supply network. In 2011, Dutch health authorities reported 476 infections with the opportunistic pathogen Legionella pneumophila causing Legionnaires’ disease, with a 10% mortality rate. This study aimed to express the risk of L. pneumophila infections using the DALY approach, combining mortality and morbidity. The critical concentrations for L. pneumophila in drinking water were estimated using reverse QMRA. Results indicated a mean critical concentration of 23.6 CFU/L for the DALY approach, highlighting the importance of including disease burden in risk assessments. The study recommends further research on the variability of key parameters to improve risk characterization.
Main Research Question: How can the risk of Legionella pneumophila infections in Dutch drinking water be assessed using the DALY approach?
Justification for SDG: This study addresses the health risks associated with drinking water quality, specifically focusing on waterborne pathogens, which directly relates to ensuring availability and sustainable management of water and sanitation for all.
Keywords: drinking water quality, emerging pathogens, DALY, reverse QMRA
Specific Methods: reverse QMRA

Title: The path to groundwater sustainability for lithium mining: the Salar de Atacama, Chile
Abstract/Summary: The Salar de Atacama was used as a case study to understand to what extent freshwater pumping for lithium mining is impacting groundwater sustainability. The aim was to evaluate both aquifer governance and performance components. The methodology included the groundwater balance method and the water table fluctuation method. Findings indicate that while the average recharge rate is superior to pumping, indicating renewable groundwater availability, aquifer governance is weak, undermining overall sustainability. Recommendations include implementing a river basin management plan that recognizes indigenous community needs and expanding the monitoring network.
Main Research Question: To what extent is freshwater pumping for lithium mining impacting groundwater sustainability in the Salar de Atacama?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on groundwater sustainability and the impacts of freshwater pumping on water resources in a critical ecosystem.
Methodology Type: Qualitative Empirical Research, Quantitative Empirical Research
Specific Methods: Groundwater balance method, Water table fluctuation method

Title: BioSealing A Single Hole Fractured Rock
Abstract/Summary: Climate change poses a threat to freshwater availability around the world, especially in dryland regions. This study investigates the BioSealing technique as a potential solution to groundwater shortages in Yunnan province, China. A sandbox experiment was conducted to assess the effectiveness of BioSealing in sealing fractured rock. The results indicated a decline in effluent flow rate, suggesting some effectiveness of the technique, although the extent of its impact remains uncertain due to unexpected experimental conditions. The findings contribute to the understanding of BioSealing and bioclogging literature.
Main Research Question: To what extent is the BioSealing technique effective in sealing a single hole fractured rock to establish a groundwater table in the overlying soil?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses groundwater shortages, which directly relates to the goal of ensuring availability and sustainable management of water and sanitation for all.
Keywords: BioSealing, groundwater, Yunnan, Climate Change, fractured rock
Specific Methods: Sandbox experiment, bacterial count analysis, dual gamma-ray transmission method, 5TE sensors

Title: Precipitation and guano deposition: their combined impact on vegetation dynamics
Abstract/Summary: Coastal ecosystems provide vital ecosystem services, including habitat provision, nutrient cycling, and shoreline protection. Vegetation helps to sustain these functions by acting as ecosystem engineers through soil stabilisation, nutrient flows regulation and food web support. This research studies the influence of precipitation on vegetation productivity, focusing on the role of guano-derived nitrogen and plant community composition. It was hypothesised that precipitation positively affects vegetation productivity, moderated by guano input and plant communities. A study was conducted on five uninhabited islands in the Dutch Wadden Sea. NDVI anomalies were significantly influenced by precipitation anomalies, with vegetation responses varying across islands. Findings highlight the importance of island-specific monitoring and management to support long-term ecosystem resilience.
Main Research Question: To what extent does guano deposition influence the relationship between precipitation regimes and vegetation dynamics across spatial and temporal scales?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the interactions between precipitation, nutrient deposition, and vegetation dynamics, which are crucial for maintaining healthy terrestrial ecosystems.
Keywords: vegetation dynamics, precipitation, guano deposition, Wadden Sea, NDVI
Specific Methods: Field surveys, nitrogen isotope analysis, NDVI analysis, Generalized Additive Model (GAM)

Title: Water use and salinity dynamics in irrigated agriculture. A case study on irrigation management and crop yield under water scarcity in the Nile Delta.
Abstract/Summary: Agricultural systems in the Nile Delta are under growing pressure from climate change. Freshwater supplies are declining, river flows are becoming less reliable, and salinity stress is intensifying. This study investigates how water availability, irrigation demand, and salinity stress interact at Buzoor Farm Complex in Sharkia Governorate, Egypt. The results show that freshwater remains the key factor for sustaining yields. Across all crop rotations, current supply falls short by 18-44% over a three-year cycle. The findings suggest that drainage water can only be used safely in small amounts and in combination with freshwater. Long-term resilience will require additional storage capacity and stronger management of salinity.
Main Research Question: How do water availability, irrigation demand, and salinity stress interact at Buzoor Farm Complex in Sharkia Governorate, Egypt?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the study focuses on water management and the challenges posed by salinity in irrigation, which directly impacts water quality and availability for agriculture.
Specific Methods: CROPWAT/CLIMWAT model, Maas-Hoffman salinity framework

Title: Protecting the flood-prone areas outside the dikes of Dordrecht | Cost-Benefit Analysis of possible technically suitable, legally feasible, economically affordable and socially accepted solutions
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates solutions to protect flood-prone areas outside the dikes of Dordrecht, Netherlands. The aim is to identify technically suitable, legally feasible, economically affordable, and socially accepted options through a cost-benefit analysis. The methodology includes semi-structured expert interviews and analysis of various project alternatives. The findings highlight the importance of integrating technical, legal, economic, and social factors in flood risk management to enhance safety and resilience in vulnerable areas.
Main Research Question: What are the technically suitable, legally feasible, economically affordable, and socially accepted solutions to protect the flood-prone areas outside the dikes of Dordrecht?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on enhancing urban resilience and safety in flood-prone areas, contributing to sustainable urban development.
Keywords: Cost-Benefit Analysis, Flood Risk Management, Dordrecht, Water management
Specific Methods: Semi-structured interviews, Cost-benefit analysis

Title: Adaptation Tipping Points and Pathways | Towards a long-term perspective on the solution space of coastal adaptation in the Southwestern Delta in the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: This thesis addresses the pressing challenge of sea-level rise (SLR) for coastal adaptation in the Southwestern Delta of the Netherlands. The aim is to develop a systematic approach to identify Adaptation Tipping Points (ATP’s) and evaluate their decisiveness for long-term adaptation strategies. The methodology combines data from a Deltares database, literature review, and qualitative interviews with experts. Key findings indicate that specific ATP’s pose significant risks to existing management strategies, necessitating additional adaptation measures to maintain functionality in the face of SLR. The outcomes provide valuable insights for policymakers in enhancing coastal adaptation planning.
Main Research Question: Which adaptation tipping points (ATP’s) and adaptation decisions are most decisive for the solution space for coastal adaptation to SLR, focussing on the Southwestern Delta and the management targets here for the sandy coastlines, flood risks and freshwater supply?
Justification for SDG: The thesis focuses on adaptation strategies to combat the impacts of sea-level rise, which is a critical aspect of climate change and its effects on coastal regions.
Keywords: Adaptation Tipping Points, Coastal Adaptation, Sea-Level Rise, Southwestern Delta, Delta Management
Specific Methods: Interviews, Literature review

Title: Spatial and temporal analysis in land and water productivity using WaPOR data
Abstract/Summary: The Ethiopian government faces a challenge in which they aim to increase the yields of the sugarcane fields of Metahara, while decreasing the water consumption. Hence, this research analyzed the spatial and temporal distribution of land and biomass water productivity in Metahara. WaPOR data covering 2009-2021 was used to analyze five irrigation scheme performance indicators: land and water productivity, productivity gaps, water consumption, uniformity, and adequacy. The WaPOR data was compared to ground-level data for validation. Stakeholders were consulted to understand the results and the results of the WaPOR data were compared to two other variables: soil texture and the influence of the saltwater of Lake Basaka. The temporal results from this research revealed that water productivity decreased over time, and that droughts explained partly for temporal variations.
Main Research Question: How does the spatial and temporal distribution of land and water productivity in the sugarcane plantations of Metahara relate to the influence of Lake Basaka, soil texture, and droughts?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the challenge of water consumption in agriculture, specifically in sugarcane production, which is crucial for ensuring sustainable water management.
Keywords: WaPOR, water productivity, sugarcane, Ethiopia, remote sensing
Specific Methods: Performance assessment indicators analysis, Stakeholder interviews

Title: Distribution of Nature-Based Solutions in Dutch Cities: Is it Strategic and Fair?
Abstract/Summary: Cities worldwide face increasing risks from urban flooding and heat stress. In response, Dutch municipalities are implementing NBS. However, deployment has often been opportunistic and socially uneven, potentially neglecting the most vulnerable areas. This research quantitatively assesses whether the distribution of NBS in Amsterdam, Rotterdam, and Utrecht is both strategic and equitable. The study utilizes a neighborhood-level analysis, integrating geospatial data from ClimateScan.nl, climate risk maps from the Klimaateffectatlas, and socio-economic statistics from CBS. The results reveal strong spatial inequality and a pronounced Matthew Effect, with NBS expansion concentrated in already well-served neighborhoods and under-served areas facing a clear barrier to entry.
Main Research Question: Is the distribution of Nature-Based Solutions in Dutch cities strategic and equitable?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses urban climate adaptation practices, focusing on the equitable distribution of Nature-Based Solutions in cities, which aligns with the goal of making cities inclusive, safe, resilient, and sustainable.
Keywords: Nature-Based Solutions, urban climate adaptation, spatial equity, climate vulnerability, socio-economic status
Specific Methods: Neighborhood-level analysis, Multiple Linear Regression, Quantile Regression

Title: The role of Hoogheemraadschap de Stichtse Rijnlanden in urban climate adaptation
Abstract/Summary: To keep urban areas in the Netherlands inhabitable in the changing climate where extreme rainfall, drought, and heat occur increasingly often, they need to become more climate adaptive. The Deltaprogramma Ruimtelijke Adaptatie (DPRA) requires safety regions to carry out a climate stress test for locations that are at risk of water nuisance, drought, heat stress and flood risk. This research has focussed mostly on the collaboration between municipalities and water authorities. In depth research on the collaboration of Hoogheemraadschap de Stichtse Rijnlanden (HDSR) and fourteen municipalities within its management area has been conducted with the aim to provide HDSR with a set of actions they can take to assist municipalities in the creation of climate adaptation policy and the implementation of adaptation measures.
Main Research Question: How can Hoogheemraadschap de Stichtse Rijnlanden assist municipalities in the creation of climate adaptation policy and the implementation of adaptation measures?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on making urban areas more climate adaptive and improving collaboration between different government levels to achieve climate adaptation goals.
Keywords: urban climate adaptation, regional water authorities, municipal adaptation, multiple level governance
Specific Methods: Interviews, Policy analysis

Title: Preventing hydrological drought in the Pleistocene uplands: A modeling experiment with nature and managed aquifer recharge
Abstract/Summary: The summer of 2018 was one of the driest in the Netherlands, affecting water management infrastructure. This thesis studies the effects of managed aquifer recharge (MAR) for hydrological drought prevention in the Pleistocene uplands. The research question is: What are the regional effects of locally applied managed aquifer recharge as a measure to prevent hydrological drought and its impacts on natural and agricultural ecosystems in the Pleistocene uplands of the Netherlands? A combined groundwater and soil-vegetation-atmosphere model was built to analyze different MAR strategies. Results indicate that MAR can prevent hydrological drought and increase transpiration, with varying effects based on the location of recharge wells.
Main Research Question: What are the regional effects of locally applied managed aquifer recharge as a measure to prevent hydrological drought and its impacts on natural and agricultural ecosystems in the Pleistocene uplands of the Netherlands?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on improving water availability and management to prevent drought, which is crucial for sustainable water resources.
Keywords: Managed Aquifer Recharge, hydrological drought, Pleistocene uplands, groundwater, modeling
Specific Methods: Modeling, Scenario analysis

Title: IN SEARCH OF THE WATER-WISE MEGACITY Assessing current and future challenges for sustainable water management in the city of Toronto
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the challenges of sustainable water management in Toronto, aiming to assess current practices and future demands. The research employs a mixed methodology, including literature review and expert interviews, to analyze the city's water system and stakeholder involvement. Key findings indicate significant trends in water use and governance capacity, highlighting the need for integrated water resource management to address future challenges.
Main Research Question: What are the current and future challenges for sustainable water management in the city of Toronto?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on sustainable water management practices and the challenges faced by urban water systems in Toronto.
Keywords: sustainable water management, Toronto, urban water system, governance capacity, water demand
Specific Methods: Literature review, Expert interviews

Title: An Assessment Tool for Microclimate Management to Enhance Ethiopian Crop Production
Abstract/Summary: Ethiopia’s economy heavily depends on traditional rain-fed agriculture. However, the Guba Lafto, Amhara region in Ethiopia is highly susceptible to climate change and has a fragile highland ecosystem threatened by land degradation. This results in crop productivity being about 1/3 of the potential. This yield gap and climate change threats highlight the importance of building climate change resilience and increasing agricultural capacity. Effectively managing the microclimate can help increase a farm’s resilience to climate change. Therefore, this study aims to create an effective and valid assessment tool that assists Ethiopian watershed managers in guiding land and water management practices to improve microclimate conditions for optimal crop production. The results of this research are twofold: a scientific knowledge base on the microclimate’s workings and a valid assessment tool that guides users in improving the microclimate.
Main Research Question: How can an assessment tool be developed to assist Ethiopian watershed managers in improving microclimate conditions for optimal crop production?
Justification for SDG: The study addresses the impacts of climate change on agriculture in Ethiopia and aims to enhance resilience through effective microclimate management.
Keywords: Microclimate, Crop Production, Ethiopia, Assessment Tool, Climate Change
Specific Methods: In-depth literature study, Interviews, Focus groups

Title: Fluvial flood risk: an inundation modelling study to analyze the contribution of river embankments to flood risk downstream
Abstract/Summary: This study investigates the impact of river embankments on flood risk downstream. The objective is to evaluate how these structures, while providing protection, can inadvertently increase flood hazard further along the river. Utilizing the GLOFRIM framework, which integrates hydrological and hydrodynamic models, the research conducts a scenario-based analysis to assess expected annual damage (EAD) in the Niederrhein region. Findings indicate that higher protection levels upstream correlate with increased flood risk downstream, with potential damages estimated between 0.9 and 1.7 billion dollars. This highlights the need for incorporating such effects into large-scale risk assessments.
Main Research Question: How do river embankments contribute to flood risk downstream?
Justification for SDG: The study addresses urban flood risk management and the implications of flood protection measures on community safety.
Keywords: flood risk, inundation modelling, river embankments, GLOFRIM, expected annual damage
Specific Methods: Scenario analysis, hydrodynamic modelling, Statistical analysis

Title: Modelling Alternative Drinking Water Extraction Strategies for the Sandy Pleistocene Regions of the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: Sufficient provision of high-quality drinking water is vital to a healthy society and the prosperity of local industries, agriculture, and energy sectors. For the sandy Pleistocene regions of the Netherlands, phreatic aquifers are the primary sources of drinking water. However, future socio-economic and climatological stresses increasingly exert pressure on the sustainable utilization of these sources; resulting in an onset of groundwater depletion, drought enhancement, and degradation of groundwater-dependent ecosystems. This thesis presents an explorative regional study in pursuit of a sustainable drinking water provision system from groundwater for these regions. The approach followed the identification and conceptualization of two innovative alternative extraction strategies: decentralized extractions and seasonal extractions. The results showed that decentralization can have a beneficial effect on regional groundwater levels.
Main Research Question: Which alternative strategies for groundwater extraction are viable and how do they affect the regional hydro-environment in the sandy Pleistocene regions of the Netherlands?
Justification for SDG: The study focuses on sustainable drinking water provision and the management of groundwater resources, which directly relates to ensuring availability and sustainable management of water.
Keywords: Drinking water, Sustainability, Groundwater, Extraction strategies, Pleistocene uplands
Specific Methods: Scenario analysis, Geohydrological modeling

Title: Towards regional upscaling of subirrigation as a tool for agricultural climate adaptation
Abstract/Summary: This research investigates the feasibility of subirrigation as a climate adaptation strategy for agriculture in North-Limburg, the Netherlands. The study aims to develop a water balance model using Vensim to assess the water management challenges associated with regional implementation of subirrigation. The findings indicate that while subirrigation can enhance groundwater levels, it also poses risks to surface water availability during droughts. The model successfully simulates various scenarios, revealing the complexities of water distribution and the need for careful management in agricultural practices.
Main Research Question: How can the hydrological processes at the field scale, and the impact of subirrigation on these processes, be translated into a simplified qualitative field scale water balance at both field and regional scale?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the impacts of climate change on agricultural practices and explores adaptation strategies to enhance resilience against droughts.
Keywords: Agricultural Climate Adaptation, Subirrigation, Mariapeel, Vensim, System Dynamics Modelling, Regional Upscaling
Specific Methods: System dynamics modelling

Title: Developing an assessment framework for circularity in dike reinforcement projects
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the development of an assessment framework aimed at enhancing circularity in dike reinforcement projects. The objective is to create a structured approach that evaluates the circularity of materials and processes used in these projects. A qualitative empirical research methodology is employed, involving case studies and stakeholder interviews to gather insights. The findings reveal key indicators for assessing circularity, emphasizing the importance of resource management and future-proofing strategies in dike reinforcement.
Main Research Question: How can an assessment framework be developed to evaluate circularity in dike reinforcement projects?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on improving resource efficiency and promoting sustainable practices in infrastructure projects, aligning with the goal of responsible consumption and production.
Keywords: circularity, dike reinforcement, assessment framework, resource management
Specific Methods: Case study, Stakeholder interviews

Title: Mapping Drought Risk in the European Union: A Framework for Future Risk Assessment
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the topic of drought risk in the European Union, aiming to develop a comprehensive framework for assessing future drought risks. The methodology includes mapping drought risk through quantifying hazard, exposure, and vulnerability, followed by validation in the Netherlands. The main findings indicate significant variations in drought risk across EU countries and highlight the importance of localized assessments for effective risk management.
Main Research Question: How can a comprehensive framework for assessing future drought risk in the European Union be developed and validated?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the urgent need for effective climate action by assessing and mapping drought risks, which are exacerbated by climate change.
Keywords: drought risk, EU, risk assessment, Climate Change, vulnerability
Specific Methods: Mapping drought risk, Case study, Validation

Title: Beyond Implementation: Understanding Long-Term Success of WASH Projects in Kenya
Abstract/Summary: This study investigates the long-term effectiveness and sustainability of Water, Sanitation, and Hygiene (WASH) projects implemented in Kenya, focusing on functionality, user experience, public health, and socio-economic outcomes. Using a mixed-methods approach, the research evaluates 11 projects by Vitens Evides International (VEI) and Water for Life (WfL) in Kisumu and Nakuru counties, applying the FIETS sustainability framework alongside the F-Diagram and Sustainable Livelihoods Framework (SLF). Quantitative and qualitative data from around 2,000 survey respondents and focus group discussions reveal that shared infrastructure generally outperforms public assets, with higher scores in both functionality and user experience. The study finds links between infrastructure reliability and improvements in public health, education, and economic participation, particularly among women and children. However, challenges remain due to governance gaps, uneven performance of sanitation systems, and financial sustainability issues.
Main Research Question: What are the overall effectiveness and long-term impacts of WASH projects in Kenya on infrastructure functionality, user experience, public health, and socio-economic impacts?
Justification for SDG: The study addresses the critical need for sustainable management of water and sanitation services, which is essential for public health and socio-economic development in Kenya.
Keywords: WASH, FIETS, Sustainable Livelihoods Framework, public health, socio-economic impact
Specific Methods: Survey, Focus groups, Field observations
Theories: FIETS Framework, F-Diagram, Sustainable Livelihoods Framework
Organizations Studied: Vitens Evides International, Water for Life

Title: Computational analysis of water shortage mitigation measures in the IJsselmeer region
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates water shortage mitigation measures in the IJsselmeer region, aiming to analyze the effectiveness of various strategies. The research employs computational analysis to evaluate the impacts of increasing water levels and the supply capacity from the Amsterdam-Rhine Canal. Key findings indicate that specific measures can significantly enhance freshwater supply, addressing the challenges posed by water scarcity in the region.
Main Research Question: What are the effective water shortage mitigation measures in the IJsselmeer region?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on enhancing freshwater supply and addressing water scarcity issues in the IJsselmeer region.
Keywords: water shortage, IJsselmeer, mitigation measures, freshwater supply, Amsterdam-Rhine Canal
Specific Methods: Computational analysis

Title: Final master’s thesis - master Water Science and Management
Abstract/Summary: Increasing temperatures and changing precipitation patterns due to climate change are affecting the fresh water availability and causing drought issues globally. This thesis focuses on analysing the effect of drought mitigation measures, specifically reprofiling watercourses, on groundwater levels in the elevated sandy soils of The Netherlands. The groundwater model Amigo was applied to the study area Winterswijk to assess the impact of reprofiling primary and secondary watercourses. The findings indicate that raising the river bed and water level of both types of watercourses simultaneously is the most effective strategy for increasing groundwater levels and mitigating drought. Further research is needed to balance drought mitigation with potential nuisance from reprofiling measures.
Main Research Question: How do drought mitigation measures, specifically reprofiling watercourses, affect groundwater levels in the elevated sandy soils of The Netherlands?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the critical issue of fresh water availability and drought mitigation, which directly relates to ensuring access to water and sustainable management of water resources.
Keywords: Drought, Mitigation, Redesigning, Water system, Groundwater, Modelling
Specific Methods: Groundwater modelling, Spatial analysis, Time series analysis

Title: Sorptive transport of atmospherically deposited PFAS in selected Dutch soils.
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the sorptive transport of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) deposited from the atmosphere into selected Dutch soils. The aim is to understand the mechanisms of PFAS transport and their environmental implications. A combination of fieldwork and laboratory analysis was employed to collect data on soil properties and PFAS concentrations. The findings reveal significant variations in PFAS retention and transport across different soil types, highlighting the need for tailored management strategies for PFAS contamination in the environment.
Main Research Question: What are the mechanisms of sorptive transport of atmospherically deposited PFAS in selected Dutch soils?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the contamination of water resources by PFAS, which poses risks to human health and the environment, aligning with the goal of ensuring availability and sustainable management of water and sanitation for all.
Keywords: PFAS, transport, soils, environmental fate, contamination
Methodology Type: Qualitative Empirical Research, Quantitative Empirical Research
Specific Methods: Fieldwork, Soil physical, chemical analysis, Transport modelling

Title: Implementing and upscaling the “Improved Drainage System”
Abstract/Summary: This master thesis explores the implementation of the Improved Drainage System (VDS) in the agricultural area of the Haarlemmermeer polder, aiming to reduce freshwater demand and mitigate waterlogging risks while maintaining suitable water quality and salinity for agricultural functions. The study investigates the effect of VDS compared to traditional water management in response to climate change and autonomous salinisation. The STOWA model was utilized for this study, indicating that VDS reduces freshwater demand and can be applied in specific areas of the polder while maintaining agricultural functionality. However, it also leads to higher surface water salinity compared to traditional management.
Main Research Question: How does the implementation of the Improved Drainage System (VDS) affect freshwater demand and water quality in the agricultural areas of the Haarlemmermeer polder?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on improving water management practices to ensure sustainable water use and quality in agricultural areas, directly contributing to the goal of clean water and sanitation.
Keywords: Flexible water level, STOWA Water Balance, Internal autonomous salinisation, Sustainability, Deep polder area, Haarlemmermeer
Specific Methods: STOWA model

Title: Urban Climate Adaptation in Water Management Policy: Implementation of Measures at Regional Water Authority Zuiderzeeland
Abstract/Summary: The climate is changing, and the impact of these changes is affecting countries and communities globally. In 2018 the Delta Plan on Spatial Adaptation was created with the aim of establishing a climate-proof and water-robust country. This thesis aims to analyze what the role of regional water authorities is in the process of climate adaptation, and what factors and measures account for an effective climate adaptation policy to help ensure a water robust and climate proof urban environment. This thesis combines results from a literature study on climate adaptation measures and their successful implementation with a case study of the current situation at regional water authority (RWA) Zuiderzeeland.
Main Research Question: What is the role of regional water authorities in the process of climate adaptation, and what factors and measures account for an effective climate adaptation policy?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the thesis focuses on climate adaptation measures and the role of water authorities in addressing climate change impacts.
Keywords: climate adaptation, Water management, regional water authority, Zuiderzeeland
Specific Methods: Literature study, Case study, Semi-structured interviews

Title: How did water management and governance evolve in the city of Amsterdam from the year 1672 to the present and what can be learned from this to enhance a city’s ability to address its water challenges?
Abstract/Summary: This study investigates the evolution of water management and governance in Amsterdam from 1672 to the present. The aim is to understand historical developments and their implications for future water management. A retrospective analysis is conducted using the City Blueprint Approach, literature review, and interviews. The findings reveal that Amsterdam's water infrastructure has often been shaped by crises and international knowledge transfer. The study emphasizes the need for long-term planning and integrated approaches in water management, highlighting the importance of cooperation and knowledge exchange with other cities.
Main Research Question: How did water management and governance evolve in the city of Amsterdam from the year 1672 to the present and what can be learned from this to enhance a city’s ability to address its water challenges?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the study focuses on improving water management and governance, which are essential for ensuring sustainable access to clean water in urban areas.
Keywords: Integrated Water Resources Management, City Blueprint, Amsterdam, Retrospective analysis
Specific Methods: Retrospective analysis, Literature study, Expert interviews, Comparative analysis
Theories: Integrated Water Resources Management, Water governance, Leapfrogging

Title: Soil Sorption Experiments for Amphetamine-Type Substances
Abstract/Summary: Dumping of illicit drug production waste, namely amphetamine-type substances (ATS), is an issue that Dutch authorities continually grapple with due to the concentrated production of such drugs in the Netherlands. This thesis set out to perform an array of soil sorption experiments that would provide empirical data that enhances the understanding of how these chemicals behave in the soil system. Sorption experiments were performed using a soil sample collected from the Utrechtse Heuvelrug, focusing on five ATS chemicals. The results indicate that the sorption of MDMA, methamphetamine, and APAAN is adequately described by the Freundlich model, while data for BMK was insufficient for calculation. Overall, this thesis provides valuable insights into the environmental fate of ATS drug waste chemicals in Dutch soil.
Main Research Question: How do amphetamine-type substances behave in the soil system, particularly in terms of their sorption characteristics?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the contamination of groundwater by amphetamine-type substances, which is critical for ensuring clean water resources.
Specific Methods: Sorption experiments, LCMS analysis

Title: Analysis of the added value of remote sensing soil moisture products for near-future inundation risk assessment in North Brabant, the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: This study investigates the added value of remote sensing soil moisture products for inundation risk assessment in North Brabant, the Netherlands. The aim is to evaluate the reliability and accuracy of available soil moisture products and their relationship with precipitation, groundwater levels, and discharges. The methodology includes statistical comparisons of two soil moisture products, OWASIS and VanderSat, against in-situ measurements. Findings indicate that while VanderSat shows moderate to strong correlations with in-situ data, OWASIS is unreliable for inundation risk assessment. The study concludes that current remote sensing products are not feasible for improving inundation risk assessments in the region.
Main Research Question: How reliable and accurate are remote sensing soil moisture products for inundation risk assessment in North Brabant, the Netherlands?
Justification for SDG: The study focuses on improving water management and understanding soil moisture dynamics, which are crucial for effective water resource management and flood risk assessment.
Keywords: remote sensing, soil moisture, inundation risk assessment, Water management, OWASIS, VanderSat
Specific Methods: Statistical analysis, Correlation analysis
Organizations Studied: HydroLogic, VanderSat, Waterschap Aa en Maas

Title: Design and Construction of an Experimental Setup for BioSealing Application in Artificial Groundwater Recharge
Abstract/Summary: Water is essential for human life and nature, with its availability impacting economic development and welfare. Climate change is increasing water supply variability while demand rises, making sufficient water supply a major challenge. This study presents the BioSealing technique developed by Deltares, which uses microorganisms to seal the subsurface and enhance water retention. An experimental setup was designed to demonstrate this technique's efficiency in a laboratory setting. The artificial recharge simulation showed that the setup effectively monitored key soil parameters, contributing to addressing societal issues like droughts exacerbated by climate change.
Main Research Question: How effective is the BioSealing technique in enhancing artificial groundwater recharge?
Justification for SDG: The study addresses water scarcity and aims to improve water retention techniques, contributing to sustainable water management.
Keywords: BioSealing, artificial recharge, water tank experiment, 2D scale
Specific Methods: Laboratory setup, water flow simulation, monitoring soil parameters

Title: A Rainwater Harvesting System based Blueprint for Excess Runoff Management on Caribbean Small Island Developing States
Abstract/Summary: This research investigates the feasibility of implementing a modular rainwater harvesting system, specifically the Rigofill system, combined with new dam structures at the Coral Estate Resort in Curacao. The aim is to manage excess runoff and provide a sustainable freshwater supply. The methodology includes data collection, literature review, and application of the Curve Number method to assess potential runoff. Findings indicate that the Rigofill system can effectively capture and store rainwater, reducing flooding risks and enhancing water availability during dry periods.
Main Research Question: To what extent can the implementation of Rigofill provide a solution for the excess runoff at the Coral Estate Resort, plus account for its own freshwater supply; and what is the potential for implementation of this type of RWH system on other SIDS?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses water scarcity and management issues in Small Island Developing States, aligning with the goal of ensuring availability and sustainable management of water and sanitation for all.
Keywords: Rainwater Harvesting, Rigofill, Excess Runoff, Curacao, SIDS
Specific Methods: Curve Number Method, Data Collection, Literature review

Title: Modelling pollution hot spots as a result of industrial effluent in Dhaka, Bangladesh
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the pollution hot spots in Dhaka, Bangladesh, caused by industrial effluent. The aim is to model the impact of various industries on water quality and identify critical areas of pollution. A combination of scenario analysis and validation techniques is employed to assess the extent of pollution and its sources. The findings reveal significant variations in pollution levels across different industrial sectors, highlighting the urgent need for improved environmental management and regulatory measures.
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on water quality issues resulting from industrial pollution, which directly impacts access to clean water.
Specific Methods: Scenario analysis, Modelling, Validation/Calibration
Flag: Missing Research Question, Abstract too short

Title: Impact of land subsidence on the population and land use in the Mekong delta
Supervisor: Hans Middelkoop, P. S. J. Minderhoud, Murray Scown
Abstract/Summary: This research investigates the impact of land subsidence on the population and land use in the Mekong delta, aiming to improve the Shared Socio-economic Pathways (SSP) scenarios at a regional scale. The study employs the IMAGE model to project land use and population density while incorporating local environmental feedback from land subsidence. The findings reveal significant discrepancies between SSP projections and actual land use opportunities, with a substantial portion of the delta projected to be below sea level by 2100. Recommendations for incorporating local feedback into global models are provided.
Main Research Question: What is the impact of land subsidence on the projections of the SSP models for the Mekong delta region and its inhabitants?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the impacts of climate change, specifically sea level rise and land subsidence, on vulnerable populations in the Mekong delta, highlighting the need for climate action.
Keywords: land subsidence, Mekong delta, Shared Socio-economic Pathways, Climate Change, population density
Specific Methods: Case study, Scenario analysis, Policy analysis

Title: Plausibility of pathways for landscape development in peat meadows: An exploration of the Amstelscheg case study
Abstract/Summary: This study investigates the plausibility of pathways for landscape development in the Green Heart, focusing on the Amstelscheg case study. The aim is to identify sustainable strategies to address soil subsidence caused by peat oxidation. A stakeholder analysis was conducted, and semi-structured interviews were held to gather insights on future visions for peatlands. The findings indicate that a production landscape scenario, which incorporates nature-inclusive agriculture, is preferred by stakeholders. The study highlights the importance of an adaptation pathway approach to guide policymakers in implementing effective measures against soil subsidence.
Main Research Question: What could be plausible pathways for addressing soil subsidence in peatland areas in the Green Heart?
Justification for SDG: The study addresses soil subsidence and greenhouse gas emissions in peat meadows, which are critical issues in the context of climate change and environmental sustainability.
Keywords: peat meadows, soil subsidence, adaptation pathways, stakeholder analysis, Green Heart
Specific Methods: Semi-structured interviews, Stakeholder analysis, Multi-criteria decision analysis

Title: Pressing The Accelerator For Urban Climate Adaptation: Identifying Critical Factors For Success Among Europe’s Frontrunner Cities
Abstract/Summary: Contemporary cities face common trends that are bringing new challenges and opportunities. Water-related climate change hazards, like pluvial flooding and drought, alongside demographic change and urbanization, require adaptation to create healthy, sustainable cities. The objective of this research is to compare and identify critical factors for accelerating adaptation success in frontrunner cities. Using the concept of adaptive capacity as an analytical frame, a qualitative assessment of adaptation implementation in London, Copenhagen, Rotterdam, Berlin and Warsaw was undertaken. Comparative analysis showed that inspirational leadership, horizontal coordination, financial availability, adaptation expertise and community initiatives were strong enabling factors for adaptation success.
Main Research Question: What are the critical factors for accelerating urban climate adaptation success in frontrunner cities?
Justification for SDG: This research focuses on enhancing urban resilience and sustainability in the face of climate change, aligning with the goal of making cities inclusive, safe, resilient, and sustainable.
Keywords: urban climate change adaptation, water-related hazards, adaptive capacity, actionability
Specific Methods: Semi-structured interviews, Content analysis

Title: Quantifying historical impact of groundwater irrigation practices to the ecological environment and the availability of the High Plains Aquifer
Abstract/Summary: This research investigates the historical impacts of groundwater irrigation practices on the ecological environment and the sustainable availability of the High Plains Aquifer from 1901 to 2018. The aim is to quantify the volumetric changes in groundwater and assess the environmental flow requirements. A hydrologic model, PCR-GLOBWB, is employed for quantitative analysis, alongside validation techniques. The findings reveal significant trends in groundwater depletion and highlight the need for sustainable management practices to ensure the aquifer's longevity.
Main Research Question: What are the historical impacts of groundwater irrigation practices on the ecological environment and the sustainable availability of the High Plains Aquifer?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on groundwater management and sustainable practices to ensure the availability of water resources.
Keywords: Groundwater, Irrigation, High Plains Aquifer, Environmental Flow, Hydrologic Model
Specific Methods: Hydrologic model: PCR-GLOBWB, Zonal analysis, Validation techniques
Theories: Groundwater footprint, Environmental flow requirements, Hydrologic model: PCR-GLOBWB

Title: Getting Inspired: Applying other countries’ drought measures to the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: Water shortage is a globally occurring phenomenon whose economic, social, and environmental impacts are the product of the meteorological and hydrological fluctuations and the influence of society on this climate variability. Future climate scenarios indicate that more severe drought may become a natural part of the Netherlands’ climate. This research applies an international social learning approach to suggest improvements to the drought management of Water Authority Hoogheemraadschap de Stichtse Rijnlanden (HDSR). Seventeen drought measures from other countries were collected and assessed for their applicability through focus-group discussions. The ten resulting measures require further research and piloting, and are extrapolatable to most other Water Authorities in the Netherlands.
Main Research Question: What can Hoogheemraadschap de Stichtse Rijnlanden learn from other countries’ drought management measures?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on improving water management and resilience to drought, which directly impacts water availability and quality.
Keywords: drought management, social learning, water authority, international measures, resilience
Specific Methods: Focus groups, Explorative research

Title: Social Cost-Benefit Analysis of Mangrove Restoration in Mozambique
Abstract/Summary: The people of Icidua, a neighbourhood of Quelimane (Mozambique), rely on logging their local mangrove population for their livelihood to survive. Unfortunately, these same practises are causing environmental degradation, the erosion of their natural riverbanks, resulting in floods which wash away their homes. This study assessed potential alternative livelihoods to promote community participation in mangrove restoration efforts. Two policy plans were compared: Business as Usual and Alternative Livelihood Development. Results showed a net positive economic impact of the Alternative Livelihood Development policy, indicating the necessity of including alternative livelihoods in mangrove restoration projects.
Main Research Question: How can alternative livelihoods be integrated into mangrove restoration efforts in Icidua, Mozambique, to enhance community engagement and economic viability?
Justification for SDG: This study addresses the restoration of mangrove ecosystems, which are crucial for biodiversity and combating land degradation.
Keywords: Community Engagement, Mangrove Restoration, Alternative Livelihoods, Social Cost-Benefit Analysis
Specific Methods: Systematic literature review, Baseline Household Survey, Community Consultations, Workshop, Cost-benefit analysis

Title: Water Sensitive Urban Design – Hydrological Effects of an Infiltration Trench with a Permeable Kerb in an Urban Streetscape
Abstract/Summary: Urbanisation and climate change have intensified stormwater runoff and urban flooding, demanding effective and sustainable water management solutions. Water Sensitive Urban Design (WSUD) systems, like infiltration trenches, are promising solutions for effective stormwater management. This study evaluates the hydrological effects of an infiltration trench paired with a permeable kerb inlet. Over a 7-month period in northern Melbourne, Australia, the research examines the system’s capacity to retain runoff, reduce peak flows, and enhance soil moisture through passive irrigation. The system achieved a runoff retention of 77.4% and an average peak flow reduction of 90.6%, outperforming similar systems. Findings highlight the importance of system-catchment ratio, storage volume, and unrestricted inlet configuration.
Main Research Question: What are the hydrological effects of an infiltration trench paired with a permeable kerb inlet in an urban streetscape?
Justification for SDG: This study addresses urban flooding and stormwater management, contributing to the development of sustainable urban environments.
Keywords: Water Sensitive Urban Design, infiltration trench, permeable kerb, stormwater management, urban flooding
Specific Methods: Data collection, hydrological analysis, soil moisture measurement

Title: Potential of a Suppletion Ditch for Restoration of Bog Woodland in De Wieden
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the potential of a suppletion ditch to restore bog woodland in De Wieden, a mire system in the Netherlands affected by desiccation. The objective is to model the hydrological conditions necessary for the restoration of this habitat. Using the HYDRUS-2D model, various scenarios were simulated to assess the impact of a suppletion ditch on phreatic levels and soil moisture content. The findings indicate that while winter phreatic levels may decrease slightly, summer levels can increase significantly, suggesting that a suppletion ditch can enhance hydrological conditions for bog woodland restoration.
Main Research Question: To what extent will a suppletion ditch create suitable hydrological conditions for the restoration of bog woodland based on hydrological modelling of De Wieden?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on restoring bog woodland, which is crucial for biodiversity and ecosystem health, aligning with the goal of protecting, restoring, and promoting sustainable use of terrestrial ecosystems.
Keywords: bog woodland, suppletion ditch, hydrological modelling, De Wieden, peatland
Specific Methods: HYDRUS-2D

Title: UV-LEDS FOR DRINKING WATER DISINFECTION IN THE NETHERLANDS AS AN ALTERNATIVE TO MERCURY-BASED LAMPS
Abstract/Summary: The EU is progressively restricting the use of mercury to protect human health and the environment. Consequently, Dutch water utilities must find an alternative to their mercury-based UV disinfection systems before they are permanently banned. UV-LEDs are a promising replacement. However, their feasibility for full-scale water disinfection in the Netherlands remains unclear due to questions about their germicidal performance, required surface area, energy efficiency, and cost-effectiveness. To assess whether a simulated UV-LED reactor could match the performance of a real-world UV reactor with respect to these parameters, three mathematical models based on two reactor designs were optimized in Microsoft Excel. The modeling results show that the SPR-1 is the most efficient of the reactor designs researched. In conclusion, UV-LED technology is robust enough to at least be piloted in small full-scale drinking water treatment plants in the Netherlands.
Main Research Question: How feasible are UV-LEDs as an alternative to mercury-based UV disinfection systems for full-scale water disinfection in the Netherlands?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the need for safe drinking water by exploring alternatives to mercury-based disinfection systems, aligning with the goal of ensuring availability and sustainable management of water and sanitation for all.
Keywords: UV-LEDs, drinking water, disinfection, mercury-based lamps, water treatment
Specific Methods: Mathematical modeling, benchmarking

Title: Developing a method to quantify drought via multiple indicators to analyze the freshwater goal of the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: This thesis addresses the challenge of quantifying drought in the Netherlands to evaluate the effectiveness of strategies aimed at achieving the freshwater goal. The objective is to develop a method that incorporates multiple indicators of drought, such as cumulative precipitation deficit and river discharge levels. A mixed methods approach is employed, combining literature review and empirical data analysis. The findings reveal significant regional variations in drought indicators and highlight the need for tailored strategies to enhance water resilience in the face of climate change.
Main Research Question: How can a method be developed to quantify drought using multiple indicators to assess the effectiveness of strategies for achieving the freshwater goal in the Netherlands?
Justification for SDG: This research directly contributes to ensuring availability and sustainable management of water resources, addressing the increasing challenges of water scarcity and drought in the Netherlands.
Keywords: drought, freshwater goal, indicators, Water management, Netherlands
Specific Methods: Literature review, Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis

Title: Global Changes of Flood Hazard in Cities Developing a Global Approach to Assess Hydroclimatic Changes in Cities Across a Wide Range of Futures
Abstract/Summary: As the climate changes, weather patterns are expected to shift. Stakeholders are interested in how strong and how frequent future storm events might become and what this means for the climate hazard cities face. This research aims to contribute to the climate risk assessment of cities by proposing a new approach to determine the city’s most at risk of flooding under different climate conditions. The approach moves away from conventional top-down approaches and utilizes global datasets and hydrological models. The results show the developed workflow tested on Bangkok for riverine flooding, raising discussions about methodologies for climate risk assessments and improving the reliability of global flood hazard methodologies.
Main Research Question: How can a new approach to climate risk assessment improve the identification of cities most at risk of flooding under different climate conditions?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the need for improved climate risk assessments in urban areas, contributing to the resilience of cities against climate hazards.
Keywords: Climate Risk in Cities, Flood Hazard, Global Risk Assessment, Weather Generator, Hydrological Extremes
Specific Methods: Workflow development, Hydrological modeling, Climate change narrative analysis

Title: A pro-active regional control system framework for the polder “De Eendragt” in the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the development of a proactive regional control system framework for the polder 'De Eendragt' in the Netherlands. The aim is to enhance water management strategies in response to climate change effects on weather extremes. The methodology includes a detailed analysis of current management practices and the implementation of smart water management techniques. Key findings indicate that integrating advanced data collection and decision support systems can significantly improve water management outcomes in the region.
Main Research Question: How can a proactive regional control system framework be developed for the polder 'De Eendragt' to enhance water management in the face of climate change?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the impacts of climate change on water management, aiming to develop strategies that enhance resilience and adaptation in the polder system.
Keywords: Smart water management, Climate Change, Water management, Decision support systems
Specific Methods: Case study, Policy analysis

Title: Parameterization of a Non-Linear Groundwater Storage-Discharge Relationship for PCR-GLOBWB
Abstract/Summary: Groundwater is an essential major water resource that is utilized for human water needs and is an integral part of the intricate water system. To grasp its complexity and challenges, a global groundwater model has been developed. This model predicts future groundwater patterns, especially under the influence of climate change, and serves as a risk mitigation tool in groundwater management. PCR-GLOBWB 2 is one such global hydrological model that can be coupled with the groundwater model. In this groundwater model, the linear reservoir concept is applied. However, some previous studies have indicated a non-linear relationship between baseflow and groundwater storage represents a better reality compared to the linear reservoir. Therefore, we aim to identify and apply this non-linearity using the recent groundwater model, GLOBGM. Additionally, we explore the multiple drainages and various conductance concepts to explore potential model improvements. The result of this recent advancement concept does not yet show an entire improvement. However, improvements are observed partially, especially in regions with wet or high-precipitation climates based on their Kling-Gupta Efficiency (KGE) values. In contrast, the model performs poorly in most dry climate areas, sometimes failing to simulate discharge, as seen in the River Niger, Mali, where it only shows a zero value for some periods.
Main Research Question: Can we improve PCR-GLOBWB performance with the new nonlinear storage-outflow relationship?
Justification for SDG: This research focuses on improving groundwater management and modeling, which is crucial for ensuring sustainable water resources and addressing water scarcity issues.
Keywords: Groundwater, PCR-GLOBWB, Non-linear relationship, Baseflow, GLOBGM
Specific Methods: Linear regression, Simulation, Model evaluation

Title: Navigating Extreme Rainfall: Adaptive Strategies for Flooding in Upper Bengawan Solo Sub-Watershed, Indonesia
Abstract/Summary: Fluvial flooding poses an increasing threat to downstream cities within the Upper Bengawan Solo Sub-watershed, particularly in Surakarta. This study investigates how extreme rainfall, land use change, and catchment characteristics contribute to tributary-driven flood events and proposes adaptive strategies to reduce risk at the catchment level. A mixed-methods approach was employed, combining HEC-HMS hydrological modeling with qualitative insights from expert interviews. The model simulated flood discharges for return periods, identifying key tributaries as dominant contributors. The research identifies promising adaptation strategies, though their implementation faces challenges. Effective flood risk reduction requires targeted action in high-risk tributaries, supported by institutional coordination.
Main Research Question: How can adaptive strategies be developed to mitigate the impacts of extreme rainfall and flooding in the Upper Bengawan Solo Sub-watershed?
Justification for SDG: This study addresses the increasing threat of flooding in urban areas, aiming to enhance resilience and sustainability in city planning and management.
Keywords: flooding, adaptive strategies, extreme rainfall, hydrological modeling, land use change
Specific Methods: HEC-HMS hydrological modeling, Expert interviews, Random Forest analysis

Title: A study on the primary sources of natural arsenic pollution in the Ganges and Indian Brahmaputra River Basins
Abstract/Summary: Arsenic is a toxic trace metal that poses serious health risks, particularly in the densely populated Ganges-Brahmaputra-Meghna delta. This study aims to identify the primary sources of arsenic pollution in the groundwater of the Ganges and Brahmaputra River Basins. A data mining exercise was conducted to gather literature on groundwater arsenic concentrations and heavy metals. The findings reveal that high arsenic concentrations are present in both river basins, with potential sources linked to geological formations in the Himalayas. The study concludes that while leucogranites may contribute to arsenic enrichment, other unknown sources also play a significant role.
Main Research Question: What are the primary sources of arsenic pollution in the groundwater of the Ganges and Brahmaputra River Basins?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the study addresses groundwater pollution, which directly impacts water quality and public health in the region.
Specific Methods: Data mining

Title: The impact of drought on water quality (nutrients) in agricultural ditches
Abstract/Summary: Surface waters in the Netherlands are being polluted by excess nitrate (NO3-) and ammonium (NH4+) compounds which mostly originate from farming. Droughts have shown to alter NO3- and NH4+ concentrations in surface water in previous studies and this could depend on the soil type. This thesis aimed to analyse the impact of droughts on NO3- and NH4+ concentrations in ditch water in the 11 Minerals Policy Monitoring Programme soil type districts. Statistical tests were performed, and a literature review was conducted. It was determined whether the water management strategy should be used in the future. The main findings reveal a significant increase in NO3- concentrations during the winter of 2019 compared to the long-term mean.
Main Research Question: How do droughts impact NO3- and NH4+ concentrations in agricultural ditches in the 11 LMM soil type districts?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on water quality in agricultural ditches, which is directly related to clean water management.
Specific Methods: Statistical analysis, Spearman’s correlation test, Superposed Epoch Analysis, Literature review

Title: Quantifying the effects of contour trenching: A field scale case study in Murcia, Spain
Abstract/Summary: This research aimed to quantify the effects of contour trenching in terms of soil moisture content and erosion rates on a hillslope scale in the region of Murcia, Spain. Fieldwork was executed to obtain the required data on local soil and infiltration characteristics that serves as input for the process of the contour trench design. Infiltration curves were constructed and statistically analysed as well as fitted to the Horton equation. The contour trench designs that best fitted local conditions were evaluated hydrologically in HYDRUS 2D. Significant increases in soil moisture content were found up to a lateral distance of 1.15 meter from the trenches. The earth mound proved to limit the lateral dispersion downslope of the trench through its capillary pull. Furthermore, the implementation of a contour trench system did not show to impact the cumulative root water uptake of an agricultural hillslope, where the loss of cultivatable surface that is the result of the implementation of the trenches is compensated by the increased soil moisture contents in the inter trench zone. Finally, a revised Morgan-Morgan Finney model was applied to evaluate the impact of contour trenches on annual erosion rates, which was shown to be reduced by 97% through the implementation of trenches.
Main Research Question: What are the effects of contour trenches on soil moisture content and erosion rates on a hillslope scale in the region of Murcia, Spain?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses soil erosion and moisture retention, which are critical for maintaining healthy ecosystems and sustainable land management.
Keywords: Contour trenching, Rainwater harvesting, Erosion, HYDRUS, Murcia (Spain)
Methodology Type: Qualitative Empirical Research, Quantitative Empirical Research
Specific Methods: Field measurements, Statistical analysis, HYDRUS modelling

Title: Managed River Realignment as a Nature Based Solution
Abstract/Summary: The Philippines is currently trying to implement viable flood control infrastructure projects to protect its citizens that reside in urbanized areas of estuaries. Recent directives from developmental agencies call for approaches to serve this function without compromising ecosystem services. This study explores the possible methods of managed river realignment as a Nature Based Solution to storm surge events and how this might perform in the face of climate change driven sea level rise. The data gathered for this study was obtained from multiple sources including remote sensing techniques for 5 separate rivers within the Philippines. Using a morphology estimator tool combined with a 1D-hydrodynamic model it was found that widening the mouth of an estuary has the capabilities of effectively dampening inland tidal amplitude associated with storm surge disturbance events. From the analysis of the results it shows a promising river management solution to flood mitigation associated with storm surge events for urbanized areas residing within estuaries.
Main Research Question: What is the impact of widening the floodplains of 6 river basins in the Philippines, (i.e the Buayan-Malungon, Jalaur, Apayo-Abulug, Abra, Ranao, and Tagum-Libuganon river basins) regarding mitigating flood risk?
Specific Methods: Field Observations, Literature review, Identification of Rivers Estuary Inhabitation “Hotspots”, Identification of Relevant NBS, Widening the Mouth of the River Basin, Initial Morphology Tool Data Input Acquisition, Validation of Morphology Tool, Initial 1D-hydrodynamic Model Input
Theories: Nature-Based Solutions, Integrated Water Resources Management, Flood Risk Management
Flag: Missing Research Question, Missing SDG, Missing Supervisor

Title: Biodegradable Groundwater Monitoring Wells: Investigating the Fate of Biowell
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the biodegradation performance of biodegradable groundwater monitoring wells, known as 'Biowell', in soil environments. The aim is to understand the fate of Biowell and its potential as a sustainable alternative to conventional plastic wells. The research employs external interviews and internal surveys to gather insights on optimal conditions for Biowell usage. Findings indicate that Biowell is suitable for short-term monitoring projects, but legislative barriers hinder its large-scale adoption. The study also reveals that Biowell experiences slow biodegradation in natural conditions, with laboratory tests suggesting a degradation time of approximately 3000 to 3500 days under varying temperatures.
Main Research Question: What is the fate of the biodegradable monitoring well?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses plastic pollution and explores sustainable alternatives, aligning with the goal of responsible consumption and production.
Keywords: Biodegradable, groundwater monitoring, Biowell, biodegradation, Sustainability
Methodology Type: Qualitative Empirical Research, Quantitative Empirical Research
Specific Methods: External interviews, Survey, chemical analysis, Py GC-MS test, soil burial tests

Title: Detecting unwanted consequences of a decreased maintenance condition in the main waterways of drainage areas using predictive machine learning techniques
Abstract/Summary: Maintaining the condition of the drainage areas is an important task of the regional water authority 'Hoogheemraadschap De Stichtse Rijnlanden', abbreviated by HDSR. HDSR prefers to monitor the maintenance condition of the drainage areas continuously in order to keep the condition at an acceptable level. However, observing this condition continuously for each drainage area is financially unfeasible. The aim of the study is to develop an approach to measure the maintenance condition of the main waterways of the drainage areas of HDSR in real time. This approach requires the water level difference in the waterways, Δh, as a parameter of the maintenance condition, since Δh is linked to flow resistance. In order to develop the approach, two case studies were performed: one in the Amerongerwetering drainage area and one in the Lange Weide drainage area. During the study, machine learning techniques, such as a linear regression model, a random forest model and a gradient boosting model, were applied. The models required a large input dataset to predict the Δh values. These value were compared to the observed values of Δh. When observing a significant difference between the predicted and the observed values, the date was classified as an anomaly. The data included in the study were provided by HDSR and KNMI. The linear regression model was not suitable for the study, because of insufficient prediction quality. Both the results of the random forest and gradient boosting model showed that most of the anomalies were detected. The anomalies were analysed and were linked to possible explanations. This analysis explained that excessive vegetation has large influence on Δh. The approach proved more promising for the (simpler) Amerongerwetering drainage area, compared to the Lange Weide drainage area. The random forest model proved to be a better performing model, both statistically and visually. The study concludes that machine learning provides opportunities for the water management in the drainage areas of HDSR. However, it is recommended that these opportunities are further examined in future studies.
Main Research Question: How can predictive machine learning techniques be used to measure the maintenance condition of the main waterways in the drainage areas of Hoogheemraadschap De Stichtse Rijnlanden?
Justification for SDG: This study focuses on improving water management and maintenance of drainage areas, which directly relates to ensuring availability and sustainable management of water and sanitation for all.
Keywords: machine learning, maintenance condition, drainage areas, predictive techniques, anomaly detection
Specific Methods: Case study, Linear regression model, Random forest model, Gradient boosting model

Title: Silicate gels in civil engineering: The effect of organic matter content of soils on the hydraulic conductivity and strength of silicate gels
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the impact of organic matter on silicate grouting, which is increasingly used in urban underground construction. The objective is to understand how varying organic mass fractions affect the syneresis, permeability, and uniaxial compressive strength of silicate gels. Through exploratory experiments, it was found that higher organic content reduces gel shrinkage and that permeability is influenced more by grouting procedures than by the grout itself. Additionally, an organic mass fraction of 8% can significantly decrease the strength of grouted soil samples.
Main Research Question: How does the organic matter content of soils affect the hydraulic conductivity and strength of silicate gels?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses urban underground construction challenges, contributing to sustainable urban development by improving grouting techniques.
Specific Methods: Syneresis experiments, Permeability experiment, UCS experiments

Title: The importance and vulnerability of the Indus regional Water Towers - Applying the global Water Tower Index at subbasin scale
Abstract/Summary: Mountains occupy a critical role in the storage and supply of sufficient freshwater to livelihoods and ecosystems in lowland areas, termed ‘water towers’. This study applies the global Water Tower Index (WTI) and Vulnerability Index (VI) at subbasin scale for the Indus Basin to determine their applicability. The research aims to identify important and vulnerable regions within the Indus Basin using regional data. Findings indicate that the global WTI and VI are applicable at subbasin scale, revealing significant variability in outcomes and improving representation of spatial variability. The regional Water Tower Index (rWTI) could serve as a preliminary tool for complex hydrological studies.
Main Research Question: Is the global Water Tower Index and Vulnerability Index applicable at subbasin scale for the Indus Basin?
Justification for SDG: This study addresses the critical role of water towers in freshwater supply and their vulnerability, directly relating to the sustainable management of water resources.
Keywords: Water Towers, Indus Basin, Water Tower Index, Vulnerability Index, Subbasin Scale
